title: Influenza vaccination in Australia
summary: Annual influenza vaccination is recommended for everyone aged 6 months and older who can receive it. It cannot cause influenza and remains worthwhile even if given after the usual autumn campaign.
evidenceSnapshot: Australia updates influenza vaccines each year. The National Immunisation Program funds vaccination for specified groups at higher risk of severe influenza.

interventions.name: Annual influenza vaccination
interventions.finding: Reduces influenza illness and, importantly, severe disease and complications.
interventions.practical: Book each year, ideally before influenza activity rises; later vaccination can still protect for the remaining season.
interventions.cautions: Protection is not immediate and varies with age, health and strain match.

interventions.name: Vaccination during pregnancy
interventions.finding: Protects the pregnant person and provides antibodies that help protect the infant after birth.
interventions.practical: Recommended in every pregnancy and can be given at any stage.
interventions.mechanism: Maternal immune response and transplacental antibodies.
interventions.cautions: Discuss timing alongside other maternal vaccines.

interventions.name: NIP-funded vaccination
interventions.finding: Improves access for groups at greater risk of severe influenza.
interventions.practical: Current funded groups include children 6 months to under 5 years, all adults 65+, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people 6 months+, pregnant people and people with specified medical conditions.
interventions.cautions: Eligibility and delivery arrangements can change; check the current Australian Immunisation Handbook or health.gov.au.

practicalSteps: Ask a GP, pharmacist or immunisation provider which vaccine formulation is appropriate.
Do not delay solely because autumn has passed; influenza can circulate later.
Allow about two weeks for the immune response to develop.
Children receiving influenza vaccine for the first time may need two doses depending on age and history; confirm with the provider.
avoidOrCare: People with a history of severe allergic reaction to a vaccine component need specialist advice.
Minor cold symptoms without fever usually do not prevent vaccination, but significant acute illness may justify rescheduling.
Vaccination does not replace staying home when infectious.

myths.claim: The flu vaccine gives you influenza.
myths.reality: Injected influenza vaccines used in Australia do not contain live influenza capable of causing flu.

myths.claim: It is too late once winter starts.
myths.reality: Vaccination can still be useful while influenza is circulating, although earlier protection is preferable.

redFlags: Signs of anaphylaxis after vaccination — call 000
A concerning or persistent reaction — contact the vaccination provider or seek medical advice
unsettled: Vaccine effectiveness varies each season because circulating strains and population immunity change.
